comment_text.sn41.3:2.4: The Brahmajālasutta (DN 1). It is referred to by name at Kd 1:1.8.8 and Vb 17:21.3, and by the list of views, numbered sixty-three at Snp 3.6:42.1 and sixty at SN 8.2:5.1 = Thag 21.1:9.1.
comment_text.sn41.3:4.13: According to the commentary, “substantialist view” (sakkāyadiṭṭhi) is the sixty-third view mentioned at Snp 3.6:42.1.
comment_text.sn41.3:7.1: Citta consistently uses the honorific ayya rather than the standard āyasmā.
comment_text.sn41.3:8.8: His reason for leaving is not explained. Perhaps he did not wish to be seen as a favorite of the famous supporter. See similar occurrences at SN 41.4:6.9 and AN 3.126:7.5

root_text.sn41.3:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ sambahulā therā bhikkhū macchikāsaṇḍe viharanti ambāṭakavane.
root_text.sn41.3:1.2: Atha kho citto gahapati yena therā bhikkhū tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā there bhikkhū abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho citto gahapati there bhikkhū etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.3:1.5: Atha kho citto gahapati therānaṁ bhikkhūnaṁ adhivāsanaṁ viditvā uṭṭhāyāsanā there bhikkhū abhivādetvā padakkhiṇaṁ katvā pakkāmi.
root_text.sn41.3:1.6: Atha kho therā bhikkhū tassā rattiyā accayena pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya yena cittassa gahapatissa nivesanaṁ tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā paññatte āsane nisīdiṁsu.
root_text.sn41.3:2.1: Atha kho citto gahapati yena therā bhikkhū tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā there bhikkhū abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ nisīdi. Ekamantaṁ nisinno kho citto gahapati āyasmantaṁ theraṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.3:2.2: “yā imā, bhante thera, anekavihitā diṭṭhiyo loke uppajjanti:
root_text.sn41.3:2.3: ‘sassato lokoti vā, asassato lokoti vā, antavā lokoti vā, anantavā lokoti vā, taṁ jīvaṁ taṁ sarīranti vā, aññaṁ jīvaṁ aññaṁ sarīranti vā, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇā’ti vā.
root_text.sn41.3:2.4: Yāni cimāni dvāsaṭṭhi diṭṭhigatāni brahmajāle bhaṇitāni;
root_text.sn41.3:2.5: imā nu kho, bhante, diṭṭhiyo kismiṁ sati honti, kismiṁ asati na hontī”ti?
root_text.sn41.3:3.1: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā thero tuṇhī ahosi.
root_text.sn41.3:3.3: tatiyampi kho citto gahapati āyasmantaṁ theraṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.3:3.4: “yā imā, bhante thera, anekavihitā diṭṭhiyo loke uppajjanti—
root_text.sn41.3:3.5: sassato lokoti vā, asassato lokoti vā, antavā lokoti vā, anantavā lokoti vā, taṁ jīvaṁ taṁ sarīranti vā, aññaṁ jīvaṁ aññaṁ sarīranti vā, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā.
root_text.sn41.3:3.6: Yāni cimāni dvāsaṭṭhi diṭṭhigatāni brahmajāle bhaṇitāni;
root_text.sn41.3:3.7: imā nu kho, bhante, diṭṭhiyo kismiṁ sati honti, kismiṁ asati na hontī”ti?
root_text.sn41.3:3.8: Tatiyampi kho āyasmā thero tuṇhī ahosi.
root_text.sn41.3:4.1: Tena kho pana samayena āyasmā isidatto tasmiṁ bhikkhusaṅghe sabbanavako hoti.
root_text.sn41.3:4.2: Atha kho āyasmā isidatto āyasmantaṁ theraṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.3:4.3: “byākaromahaṁ, bhante thera, cittassa gahapatino etaṁ pañhan”ti?
root_text.sn41.3:4.4: “Byākarohi tvaṁ, āvuso isidatta, cittassa gahapatino etaṁ pañhan”ti.
root_text.sn41.3:4.6: ‘yā imā, bhante thera, anekavihitā diṭṭhiyo loke uppajjanti—
root_text.sn41.3:4.8: imā nu kho, bhante, diṭṭhiyo kismiṁ sati honti, kismiṁ asati na hontī’”ti?
root_text.sn41.3:4.10: “Yā imā, gahapati, anekavihitā diṭṭhiyo loke uppajjanti:
root_text.sn41.3:4.11: ‘sassato lokoti vā, asassato lokoti vā, antavā lokoti vā anantavā lokoti vā, taṁ jīvaṁ taṁ sarīranti vā, aññaṁ jīvaṁ aññaṁ sarīranti vā, hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, hoti ca na ca hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā, neva hoti na na hoti tathāgato paraṁ maraṇāti vā.
root_text.sn41.3:4.12: Yāni cimāni dvāsaṭṭhi diṭṭhigatāni brahmajāle bhaṇitāni;
root_text.sn41.3:4.13: imā kho, gahapati, diṭṭhiyo sakkāyadiṭṭhiyā sati honti, sakkāyadiṭṭhiyā asati na hontī’”ti.
root_text.sn41.3:5.2: “Idha, gahapati, assutavā puthujjano ariyānaṁ adassāvī ariyadhammassa akovido ariyadhamme avinīto, sappurisānaṁ adassāvī sappurisadhammassa akovido sappurisadhamme avinīto
root_text.sn41.3:5.3: rūpaṁ attato samanupassati, rūpavantaṁ vā attānaṁ, attani vā rūpaṁ, rūpasmiṁ vā attānaṁ;
root_text.sn41.3:5.7: viññāṇaṁ attato samanupassati, viññāṇavantaṁ vā attānaṁ, attani vā viññāṇaṁ, viññāṇasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn41.3:6.2: “Idha, gahapati, sutavā ariyasāvako ariyānaṁ dassāvī ariyadhammassa kovido ariyadhamme suvinīto sappurisānaṁ dassāvī sappurisadhammassa kovido sappurisadhamme suvinīto
root_text.sn41.3:6.3: na rūpaṁ attato samanupassati, na rūpavantaṁ vā attānaṁ, na attani vā rūpaṁ, na rūpasmiṁ vā attānaṁ;
root_text.sn41.3:6.7: na viññāṇaṁ attato samanupassati, na viññāṇavantaṁ vā attānaṁ, na attani vā viññāṇaṁ, na viññāṇasmiṁ vā attānaṁ.
root_text.sn41.3:6.8: Evaṁ kho, gahapati, sakkāyadiṭṭhi na hotī”ti.
root_text.sn41.3:7.3: “Atthi, bhante, avantiyā isidatto nāma kulaputto amhākaṁ adiṭṭhasahāyo pabbajito?
root_text.sn41.3:7.6: “Kahaṁ nu kho so, bhante, āyasmā etarahi viharatī”ti?
root_text.sn41.3:7.7: Evaṁ vutte, āyasmā isidatto tuṇhī ahosi.
root_text.sn41.3:8.1: Atha kho citto gahapati āyasmato isidattassa bhāsitaṁ abhinanditvā anumoditvā there bhikkhū paṇītena khādanīyena bhojanīyena sahatthā santappesi sampavāresi.
root_text.sn41.3:8.2: Atha kho therā bhikkhū bhuttāvino onītapattapāṇino uṭṭhāyāsanā pakkamiṁsu.
root_text.sn41.3:8.3: Atha kho āyasmā thero āyasmantaṁ isidattaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn41.3:8.4: “sādhu kho taṁ, āvuso isidatta, eso pañho paṭibhāsi.
root_text.sn41.3:8.6: Tenahāvuso isidatta, yadā aññathāpi evarūpo pañho āgaccheyya, taññevettha paṭibhāseyyā”ti.
root_text.sn41.3:8.7: Atha kho āyasmā isidatto senāsanaṁ saṁsāmetvā pattacīvaramādāya macchikāsaṇḍamhā pakkāmi.
root_text.sn41.3:8.8: Yaṁ macchikāsaṇḍamhā pakkāmi, tathā pakkantova ahosi, na puna paccāgacchīti.

translation_text.sn41.3:1.1: At one time several senior mendicants were staying near Macchikāsaṇḍa in the Wild Mango Grove.
translation_text.sn41.3:1.2: Then Citta the householder went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to them,
translation_text.sn41.3:1.3: “Sirs, would the senior monks please accept tomorrow’s meal from me?”
translation_text.sn41.3:1.5: Then, knowing that the senior mendicants had consented, Citta got up from his seat, bowed, and respectfully circled them, keeping them on his right, before leaving.
translation_text.sn41.3:1.6: Then when the night had passed, the senior mendicants robed up in the morning and, taking their bowls and robes, went to Citta’s home, and sat down on the seats spread out.
translation_text.sn41.3:2.1: So he went up to them, bowed, sat down to one side, and said to the senior venerable:
translation_text.sn41.3:2.2: “Honorable Senior, there are many different views that arise in the world. For example:
translation_text.sn41.3:2.3: the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.
translation_text.sn41.3:2.4: And also the sixty-two misconceptions spoken of in “The Divine Net”.
translation_text.sn41.3:2.5: When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?”
translation_text.sn41.3:3.1: When he said this, the senior venerable kept silent.
translation_text.sn41.3:3.3: And for a third time, Citta said to him:
translation_text.sn41.3:3.4: “Honorable Senior, there are many different views that arise in the world. …
translation_text.sn41.3:3.7: When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?”
translation_text.sn41.3:3.8: And a second time and a third time the senior venerable kept silent.
translation_text.sn41.3:4.1: Now at that time Venerable Isidatta was the most junior mendicant in that Saṅgha.
translation_text.sn41.3:4.2: He said to the senior venerable,
translation_text.sn41.3:4.3: “Honorable Senior, may I answer Citta’s question?”
translation_text.sn41.3:4.6: ‘There are many different views that arise in the world …
translation_text.sn41.3:4.8: When what exists do these views come to be? When what doesn’t exist do these views not come to be?’”
translation_text.sn41.3:4.10: “Householder, there are many different views that arise in the world. For example:
translation_text.sn41.3:4.11: the cosmos is eternal, or not eternal, or finite, or infinite; the soul and the body are one and the same, or the soul is one thing, the body another; after death, a realized one still exists, or no longer exists, or both still exists and no longer exists, or neither still exists nor no longer exists.
translation_text.sn41.3:4.12: And also the sixty-two misconceptions spoken of in “The Divine Net”.
translation_text.sn41.3:4.13: These views come to be when substantialist view exists. When substantialist view does not exist they do not come to be.”
translation_text.sn41.3:5.1: “But sir, how does substantialist view come about?”
translation_text.sn41.3:5.2: “It’s when an unlearned ordinary person has not seen the noble ones, and is neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve not seen true persons, and are neither skilled nor trained in the teaching of the true persons.
translation_text.sn41.3:5.3: They regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form.
translation_text.sn41.3:5.7: consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness.
translation_text.sn41.3:5.8: That’s how substantialist view comes about.”
translation_text.sn41.3:6.1: “But sir, how does substantialist view not come about?”
translation_text.sn41.3:6.2: “It’s when a learned noble disciple has seen the noble ones, and is skilled and trained in the teaching of the noble ones. They’ve seen true persons, and are skilled and trained in the teaching of the true persons.
translation_text.sn41.3:6.3: They don’t regard form as self, self as having form, form in self, or self in form.
translation_text.sn41.3:6.7: consciousness as self, self as having consciousness, consciousness in self, or self in consciousness.
translation_text.sn41.3:6.8: That’s how substantialist view does not come about.”
translation_text.sn41.3:7.1: “Sir, where has Master Isidatta come from?”
translation_text.sn41.3:7.3: “Sir, there’s a friend of mine named Isidatta who I’ve never met. He’s gone forth from a good family in Avanti.
translation_text.sn41.3:7.6: “Sir, where is that venerable now staying?”
translation_text.sn41.3:7.7: When he said this, Isidatta kept silent.
translation_text.sn41.3:7.10: “Sir, I hope Master Isidatta is happy here in Macchikāsaṇḍa,
translation_text.sn41.3:7.11: for the Wild Mango Grove is lovely.
translation_text.sn41.3:7.12: I’ll make sure that Master Isidatta is provided with robes, almsfood, lodgings, and medicines and supplies for the sick.”
translation_text.sn41.3:7.13: “That’s nice of you to say, householder.”
translation_text.sn41.3:8.1: Then Citta, having approved and agreed with what Isidatta said, served and satisfied the senior mendicants with his own hands with delicious fresh and cooked foods.
translation_text.sn41.3:8.2: When the senior mendicants had eaten and washed their hands and bowls, they got up from their seats and left.
translation_text.sn41.3:8.3: Then the senior venerable said to Venerable Isidatta,
translation_text.sn41.3:8.4: “Isidatta, it’s good that you felt inspired to answer that question, because I didn’t.
translation_text.sn41.3:8.6: So when a similar question comes up, you should also answer it as you feel inspired.”
translation_text.sn41.3:8.7: But Isidatta set his lodgings in order and, taking his bowl and robe, left Macchikāsaṇḍa,
